pages.full_text:
We spent the night at Pennine Lodge next to St. John's Chapel.
Sunday
   We drove to Westgate + walked up a very steep hill. We went along tracks, some even, some [?eroded], + came to an absolutely beautiful woodland path which took us back down to the van. (Under 4 miles.) There was a motorbike scramble about to happen but we couldn't stay for it because we needed lunch. We passed the entrance + washing places to quite a few lead mines.
   We returned to Killhope to look at the machinery. Some of the machines work like hop-picking machines. We had lunch + came back to Pennine Lodge. Rain. The rain held off for the morning. In the afternoon it was wet.
Monday
   We went on a short walk from the nice campsite. There's a steep car ford, but the water is deep from the rains. (We slept with the skylight open + the rain didn't come in - v. pleasant.) We walked across the pretty stream using the footbridge.
   We drove on to Vindolanda - much developed since our last visit. Most fascinating are the 350 letters left over from the leaving bonfire.
pages.notes: Page number not visible in image crop. 'eroded' reading uncertain — could be 'eroded' or another word. The diary mentions 350 letters at Vindolanda — not a page number. Era estimate circa 2017-2018 based on proportional position in volume.

pages.full_text:
Unfortunately we walked out in a shower of rain + our legs got v. wet. We had coffee to warm up. There were diggers excavating with enthusiasm. We had lunch at the cafe + enjoyed the indoor museum.
Recent excavations were started in 1938, the same as the Sutton Hoo.

Charles has sent a 4th of July video from the U.S. We have driven down to beyond Manchester for the night. Brook House Farm [?Middlewich]. With more salt. There's a salt-mining museum nearby. (Until 1986
photo_captions: The most extraordinary of all Vindolanda's letters - the birthday party invitation from Claudia Severa to Sulpicia Lepidina, the wife of Vindolanda's prefect. It dates to the period A.D. 100/104 and the closing salutation, in Claudia Severa's hand, represents the earliest surviving writing by a woman.
pages.notes: Right page contains a glued-in printed postcard/photo card of a Vindolanda writing tablet (the Claudia Severa birthday party invitation letter). The text below the postcard continues in handwriting. The farm name could read 'Middlewich' but is partially cut off at the right edge. The text ends mid-sentence with '(Until 1986' suggesting the page continues on the next spread. Sutton Hoo comparison for excavation start date of 1938 noted in text. Page number not visible in image crop.
